root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Security Guide
root.summary: Front matter and full security guide for Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2. Covers the main security model: authentication (JAAS, default and alternate login IDs, login page, password management, identity-only/external access modes, customization), authorization (users/roles/groups, SIDs/FIDs, runtime client/server checks), cryptography (digests, ciphers, encrypted passwords, keys/keystores), and security caching. Also includes security for alternative clients (web services, batch, JMS, deferred processing), external user applications and user scope, and single sign-on setup for SAML/OpenID Connect across supported app servers. Additional sections address SSL and production hardening, HTTP verb permissions, custom authentication/authorization/cryptography, sanitization allowlists, and CSRF protection via referrer validation and the required `curam.referer.domains` setting. The tail of the document contains a CSRF behavior discussion for navigation scenarios plus full legal notices, copyright, trademarks, and sample code/license terms.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Front matter for the Cúram security guide. States this edition applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2. The table of contents covers Securing Cúram, including authentication, authorization, cryptography, security caching, alternative clients, external user applications, SSO, other security considerations, customization topics, and CSRF. The opening pages introduce security fundamentals: form-based authentication via JAAS, HTTPS/SSL enabled by default, and the default login flow, with a note that alternate login IDs can be configured.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Cúram security and authentication setup. Covers alternate login IDs (ExtendedUsersInfo table, static enable property, internal-user constraints, unique IDs, cache behavior), custom alternate login implementation, login page customization, JAAS login modules, password digest storage, and WebLogic/WebSphere default configurations. Also explains authentication modes: default verification, identity-only authentication, logging in AuthenticationLog, security cache behavior, case sensitivity, account/password expiry, and requirements for custom login modules and excluded administrative users.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: This section covers Cúram security fundamentals: authentication customization, external access security hooks, and custom verifications; authorization concepts including users/roles/groups, SIDs, FIDs, field-level and user-defined SIDs; runtime client/server authorization checks and logging; cryptography for ciphers and digests, with CryptoConfig.properties settings and cipher-encrypted passwords; and security data caching, including cache refresh, case sensitivity, and WebSphere caching effects on login and credential changes.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: Covers Cúram security for non-user processes and external access. Describes mandatory internal accounts (SYSTEM, DBTOJMS, WEBSVCS), their roles, and how batch processing, JMS, deferred processing, and web services authenticate/authorize. Explains external user applications, custom PublicAccessUser/ExternalAccessSecurity, user scope detection, and deployment considerations when internal/external apps share an app server. Also introduces SSO, focusing on SAML-based IdP-initiated and SP-initiated flows, ACS/TAI behavior, and LTPA2 cookie-based session handling.

children.title: Edition — pages 45-54
children.summary: This section explains Cúram SSO setup and SAML web flows on WebSphere Liberty and Kubernetes, including IdP-initiated and SP-initiated browser POST flows, ACS/TAI behavior, SAML assertions/responses, RelayState, and LTPA2 cookies. It provides Liberty server.xml and .env configuration examples for the Universal Access responsive app, plus guidance for extracting authentication attributes in the JAAS login module. It also outlines the SSO architecture components and step-by-step IBM Security Access Manager (ISAM) configuration and IdP setup.

children.title: Edition — pages 55-64
children.summary: Setup and testing guidance for Cúram SAML SSO with IBM Security Access Manager and WebSphere Liberty. Covers ISAM federation configuration steps, optional ACL policy, adding LDAP users, importing them into ISAM, and test URLs for IdP-initiated and SP-initiated flows. Also explains SAML SSO concepts and flow behavior for Cúram and Universal Access on WebSphere Application Server, including HTTP POST binding, ACS validation, LTPA2 cookie use, and browser redirect sequences.

children.title: Edition — pages 65-74
children.summary: Covers Cúram SSO setup on WebSphere and Universal Access. Includes IdP-initiated and SP-initiated SAML 2.0 Browser POST flows, ACS validation, LTPA2 cookie handling, and Universal Access login/account setup. Gives detailed WebSphere SAML SP/ACS configuration: deploying WebSphereSamlSP.ear, TAI properties, trusted realms, certificate import, session/logout property, and CORS filter/web.xml changes. Also explains Universal Access .env SSO settings, REST allowedOrigins CORS config, and an IBM Security Access Manager example plus ISAM IdP setup steps and cookbook references.

children.title: Edition — pages 75-84
children.summary: This section covers SAML SSO setup for Cúram on WebSphere, mainly with IBM Security Access Manager/IdP and Azure. It explains deploying and configuring the WebSphereSamlSP ACS app, ACS trust association interceptor properties, trusted realm and certificate setup, CORS handling, LDAP user creation/import, and testing IdP-initiated flow. It also adds SP-initiated SAML support via a custom AuthnRequestProvider and shows how to configure and test Azure as an IdP and WebSphere as the SP.

children.title: Edition — pages 85-94
children.summary: Cúram SSO configuration details for Azure and WebLogic. Covers WebSphere settings for IdP metadata, trust store certificate import, CSRF/session-cookie property adjustments, and web.xml filter mapping. Includes sample code for an SP-initiated AuthnRequestProvider implementation and deployment steps. Then explains SAML SSO concepts and flow diagrams for WebLogic Server: IdP-initiated and SP-initiated flows for both Cúram and Universal Access, including how assertions, ACS redirects, cookies, and login handling work.

children.title: Edition — pages 95-104
children.summary: Covers Cúram 8.1.2 SSO setup for Universal Access using SAML 2.0. It describes the SP-initiated login flow, then configuration steps for Oracle WebLogic Server as a SAML service provider/identity asserter, publishing and importing metadata, redirect URI setup, and required CORS settings for cross-host deployments. It also includes an IBM Security Access Manager example for SAML federation, with steps for configuring ISAM as IdP and notes about additional cookbook sections and advanced partner configuration.

children.title: Edition — pages 105-114
children.summary: Cúram security setup for WebLogic SAML 2.0 and custom authentication. Covers configuring SAML general services and service provider settings, publishing/distributing SP metadata, creating Identity Provider partners, and testing IdP- and SP-initiated SSO. Also includes loading LDAP users into OpenLDAP and IBM Security Access Manager for SSO testing. The section then explains replacing the built-in Cúram JAAS login module with a custom login module/security provider, including de-registering the old provider, implementing WebLogic SSPI/LoginModule methods, generating an MBean type, and registering the custom authenticator.

children.title: Edition — pages 115-124
children.summary: This section covers extending Cúram SAML SSO to support multifactor authentication with IBM Secure Verify Access (ISVA). It explains prerequisites, enabling AAC, publishing/reloading ISVA containers, configuring WebSEAL reverse proxy integration, creating POPs for step-up login, updating WebSEAL to show and redirect login prompts, configuring email OTP delivery and mapping rules, testing the SP-initiated MFA flow, and optionally updating AuthSvcCredential to prevent redirect loops.

children.title: Edition — pages 125-134
children.summary: This section covers Cúram security configuration: SSO with WebSphere LTPA and WebLogic WL_Token, plus OpenID Connect SSO using Microsoft Azure as provider and WebSphere as relying party. It explains when Cúram login modules add or suppress credentials, required Azure/WAS setup values, and testing OIDC SSO. It also documents security hardening: SSL defaults, secure-environment considerations, client HTML stack traces, and restricting HTTP verbs via IBM/Oracle HTTP Server. Ends with authentication customization, including login page edits and WebLogic UTF-8 username support.

children.title: Edition — pages 135-144
children.summary: Cúram security configuration and customization: case-sensitive usernames, custom authentication via `CustomAuthenticator`, identity-only auth, cache-refresh failure callbacks, and SSL disablement options for web client/WebSphere settings. It details AuthenticationLog and AuthorisationLog fields and LogAdmin API use, plus authorization setup via DMX files for roles, groups, SIDs, FIDs, and per-user roles. It also covers cryptography customization: editing `CryptoConfig.properties`, rebuilding `CryptoConfig.jar`, managing keys with `keytool`, and creating a new JCEKS keystore.

children.title: Edition — pages 145-154
children.summary: Covers Cúram security configuration beyond defaults: customizing digest settings and salt, migrating passwords via superseded digest settings, and production cryptography hardening (new keystore/secret key, secret-key rotation, protecting config files). Then it introduces external user applications: separate web app/login page, automatic login/logout scripts, and extending PublicAccessUser/ExternalAccessSecurity to implement authentication, login details, authorization, user type, role usage count, username casing, and user preferences.

children.title: Edition — pages 155-160
children.summary: This section covers Cúram security customization and CSRF behavior. It explains custom external access handling via user preferences, a UserScope interface for distinguishing internal vs external users, and how to configure each through Application.prx properties. It also describes sanitization allowlists for HTML filtering and how to customize them via files or admin UI. Finally, it details CSRF protection for web pages, centered on Referer header validation, the curam.referer.domains property, and navigation scenarios where missing Referer headers trigger redirection.

Front matter and the full “Securing Cúram” guide for Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2. Covers authentication, authorization, cryptography, security caching, alternative clients, external user applications, SSO (SAML/OpenID Connect, WebSphere/WebLogic/Liberty, ISAM/Azure/ISVA), security hardening, custom authentication/authorization, custom cryptography, external user scope handling, sanitization allowlists, and CSRF protection via Referer validation and `curam.referer.domains`.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: This node is the table of contents for the Cúram security documentation. It lists major topics in Chapter 1, including authentication, authorization, cryptography, security data caching, alternative clients, external user applications, SSO (SAML/OpenID Connect), other security considerations, and customization guides for authentication, authorization, cryptography, external user apps, sanitization, and CSRF. It also shows the main subtopics and starting pages, useful for navigating directly to relevant security configuration or customization sections.

children.title: 1 Securing Cúram
1.1 Authentication Overview
Authentication
children.summary: Explains Cúram authentication basics and how the web client uses form-based login with JAAS by default. Covers authentication as verifying user identity, secure password storage via digests, and HTTPS/SSL enabled by default. Also notes that different authentication modes can be configured through the JAAS login module, including Default Authentication, Identity Only Authentication, and External Access Security Authentication.

children.title: Authentication Architecture
children.summary: Describes the authentication architecture flow chart for user login in Cúram 8.1.2. Explains the default authentication process and notes that it can be customized for both internal and external users based on authentication requirements. Useful for understanding the overall authentication process structure and where customization is supported.

children.title: Default Authentication
children.summary: Describes Cúram’s default authentication flow: users log in with username and password via the login screen, the credentials are passed to the application server’s JAAS login module, and verified against the Cúram Users table. Notes that the Cúram username is immutable, but a configurable login ID can be used instead. If verification succeeds, the user is authenticated and added to the Cúram Security Cache to speed authorization data retrieval.

children.title: Alternate Login IDs
children.summary: Explains Cúram’s alternate login ID feature, which lets users authenticate with a configurable login ID instead of the immutable username stored in the Users table. Covers enabling via the static curam.security.altlogin.enabled property, required population of ExtendedUsersInfo, login/audit behavior, and constraints for internal users, identity-only setups, and special system accounts (SYSTEM, DBTOJMS, WEBSVCS, Unauthenticated). Also describes the ExtendedUsersInfo table structure, ways to populate it, and uniqueness requirements for mixed internal/external users.

children.title: The Login Page
children.summary: This section explains the default login page (logon.jsp) and how it supports form-based authentication with username and password fields. It notes that password creation and changes are handled by user account administration, and warns the default password logic is only for testing/demo use. It also describes adding an optional user_type field to distinguish internal vs. external users, how these values are passed to JAAS, and when logon.jsp should be customized or replaced, such as for external user client applications.

children.title: Customization of the Login Page
children.summary: This section explains how to customize the login page in Cúram. It covers configuring a custom alternate login implementation via the curam.citizenworkspace.alternate.login.implementation property and the required CitizenWorkspaceAlternateLogin class/method. It also describes the default logon.jsp login page, how username/password are handled, and how an optional user_type field distinguishes internal vs external users. It notes that logon.jsp can be replaced, especially for external user client applications.

children.title: Cúram JAAS Login Module
children.summary: Explains Cúram authentication via a JAAS login module configured in the application server and started automatically during access. Covers support for replacing or customizing default auth, multiple login modules run in server-defined order, and successful authentication across required modules. Notes password handling: internal and external user passwords are stored as digests in Users and ExternalUsers tables, and the received password is digested before comparison. Also mentions related login.jsp customization needs for automatic login, styling, and extended-character usernames.

children.title: Password Management
children.summary: Cúram passwords for internal and external users are stored in digest form in the Cúram Users and ExternalUsers tables. The JAAS login module digests the entered password before comparing it to the stored value. Digesting is one-way for security, and matching depends on using the same digest algorithm and encryption settings. This section sits within authentication setup and notes related login customization topics elsewhere, but its core focus is secure password storage and verification.

children.title: Default Configuration for WebLogic Server
children.summary: Explains default application-server security setup for Cúram. In WebLogic, the Cúram JAAS login module is the only configured authentication provider by default, so it authenticates and verifies users; if multiple providers exist, responsibility may differ and SSO/WL_Token may apply. In WebSphere, the Cúram JAAS login module is configured as a system login module for DEFAULT, WEB_INBOUND, and RMI_INBOUND, covering web services/JMS, web requests, and Java clients. Notes that Cúram typically adds user credentials in the login chain.

children.title: Default Configuration for WebSphere
children.summary: Explains Cúram’s default WebSphere security setup: the JAAS login module runs as a system login module in the DEFAULT, WEB_INBOUND, and RMI_INBOUND chains, handling web services/JMS, web requests, and Java clients. By default it adds credentials for authenticated users, so Cúram users do not need to exist in the WebSphere user registry unless curam.security.user.registry.enabled is set true. It also notes excluded administrative users (security.username and curam.db.username) that are queried against the user registry and must exist there if a non-default registry is used.

children.title: Customizing the login module
children.summary: Explains when and how to customize the Cúram JAAS login module. Recommends keeping the Cúram module in place with identity-only authentication, but notes it can be replaced by a custom JAAS module with Support approval. If replaced, users must still exist in the Cúram Users table and the custom module must trigger Security Cache updates for new users. Also describes authentication verification basics: form-based web client login, JAAS-based username/password checks, HTTPS/SSL enabled by default, and default authentication against the Cúram Users table.

children.title: Verification Process for Authentication
Default Authentication
children.summary: Explains default authentication in Cúram’s web client. It uses form-based authentication with a JAAS login module and HTTPS/SSL enabled by default, verifying the entered username and password against the Cúram Users database table. The section also notes that custom login modules can replace the Cúram JAAS module, but support should be consulted, users must still exist in the Users table, and custom modules must update the Security Cache when new users are added.

children.title: Default Verification Process
children.summary: Describes Cúram’s default login verification flow: checks username/password, account and password expiry, security cache synchronization, break-in detection, and day/time access restrictions. Notes authentication is case sensitive by default, with ambiguous case-insensitive duplicates causing failure. Authentication failures are not shown to users but both success and failure are logged in AuthenticationLog. Also covers customization via a custom authenticator and “identity only” authentication, where Cúram verifies only that the username exists and delegates full authentication to an external mechanism like LDAP or SSO.

children.title: Authentication Attempts
children.summary: This section explains Cúram authentication attempts and logging. It covers the default login verification process (username/password, account and password expiry, cache synchronization, break-in detection, and day/time restrictions), notes that authentication is case sensitive by default, and explains that failures are not shown directly to users to avoid revealing valid usernames. All attempts are logged in the AuthenticationLog table. It also introduces customization via a custom authenticator and identity-only authentication, where full verification is delegated to another mechanism such as LDAP or SSO.

children.title: Customization of Default Authentication
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram’s default authentication works and how it can be customized. It covers the default verification checks: username/password, account and password expiry, cache synchronization, break-in detection, and day/time access restrictions. It also notes case-sensitive authentication, logging of all attempts to the AuthenticationLog table, and that failures are not exposed to clients. Customization can support a mutable login ID and extra verifications via a custom authenticator. It also introduces identity-only authentication and alternative mechanisms like LDAP, SSO, or custom login modules.

children.title: Identity Only Authentication
children.summary: Explains identity-only authentication, where Cúram only verifies that the username exists in the Users table and delegates full authentication to an external mechanism such as LDAP, SSO, or a custom login module. It logs attempts with AUTHONLY for success and BADUSER for missing users, but does not set loginFailures or lastLogin. Warns that password expiry/account-enabled fields become misleading and should not be used. Notes that users still must exist in both Cúram and the external directory, including SYSTEM and DBTOJMS.

children.title: Customization of Identity Only Authentication
children.summary: Identity Only Authentication cannot be customized directly. If additional checks are needed, they must be added by implementing a custom authenticator. The section points to “Custom Verifications” for more details.

children.title: External Access Security Authentication
children.summary: Describes how external users can use custom authentication in Cúram by extending `curam.util.security.PublicAccessUser` and implementing `ExternalAccessSecurity`, which supplies required external-user information during authentication/authorization. Also covers optional custom verifications, such as security questions, which run only after built-in checks succeed and update the Users table. The section then begins the authorization overview: authorization grants or denies access to functional elements via Security Identifiers (SIDs), with data linked to users and cached by the Cúram Security Cache for performance.

children.title: Custom Verifications
children.summary: This section explains custom verifications in Cúram authentication. It says developers can add custom checks, such as security questions, to the login process after standard Cúram verification or identity assertion succeeds. If the custom code runs, authentication updates the relevant fields in the Users database table. It also briefly introduces external access security authentication, where a developer can extend PublicAccessUser and implement ExternalAccessSecurity to plug in custom authentication for external users.

Describes Cúram web client authentication verification. Covers default form-based JAAS login with HTTPS/SSL, checking username/password plus account/password expiry, security cache sync, break-in detection, and day/time restrictions; auth is case sensitive by default and all attempts are logged in AuthenticationLog without exposing failures to users. Explains customization via custom authenticators or replacing the JAAS login module (with Support, while keeping Users-table entries and cache updates). Also introduces identity-only authentication, its limits, logging statuses, and its dependence on an external auth mechanism such as LDAP or SSO.

Introduces Cúram authentication and its default form-based web client login via JAAS over HTTPS/SSL. Covers the authentication architecture, default username/password verification against the Users table, security cache updates, and configurable alternate login IDs through ExtendedUsersInfo and `curam.security.altlogin.enabled`. Also notes custom login page/JAAS module options, WebLogic/WebSphere default security setup, and the two other modes: identity-only authentication and external access support.

children.title: 1.2 Authorization Overview
Users, Roles and Groups
children.summary: Introduces Cúram authorization and external access security. It explains that custom authentication for external users can be integrated by extending `curam.util.security.PublicAccessUser` and implementing `ExternalAccessSecurity`, and that custom verifications can run after standard checks to update the Users table. It then defines authorization as granting or denying access to application functional elements via Security Identifiers (SIDs), with security data managed through Administration screens or Data Manager and cached by Cúram Security Cache for performance.

children.title: Security Identifiers (SIDs)
children.summary: Explains Cúram’s authorization model based on security identifiers (SIDs): roles map to security groups, which map to SIDs for securable elements like methods and fields. Notes that SID and role data are stored in database tables and configured via Data Manager or Administration screens. Describes minimum SIDs in the BASESECURITYGROUP and the Supergroup.sql link. Introduces SID types, especially Function Identifiers (FIDs) for facade methods, plus field-level and user-defined SIDs, and says function-level security applies to BPOs, not entity methods.

children.title: Function Identifiers (FIDs)
children.summary: Function Identifiers (FIDs) are a type of security identifier (SID) used for function-level security in Cúram. When a process method is made publicly accessible by marking it as a facade, the system generates a FID of type FUNCTION and automatically enables security for that method. Security can still be turned off at design time for a process method. This section is mainly about how FIDs are created and how they relate to secured facade methods.

children.title: Field Level Security Identifiers
children.summary: Explains field level security identifiers (Field Level SIDs), which apply authorization to specific fields on publicly accessible methods; unauthorized field values are masked as "***" at runtime. Also introduces user-defined SIDs, noting the framework can authorize custom securable elements via curam.util.security.Authorisation.isSIDAuthorised(), with LOCATION and PRODUCT as examples. The excerpt then begins runtime authorization, including client-side authorization checks before a page loads.

children.title: User Defined SIDs
children.summary: Explains user defined SIDs as custom security identifier types beyond FIDs and field-level SIDs. Notes that Cúram authorization can be extended to any securable application element using `curam.util.security.Authorisation.isSIDAuthorised()`. Mentions that LOCATION and PRODUCT are built-in examples of this type and refers to “Authorizing New SID Types” for details. Also introduces runtime authorization checks, including client-side checks before page load.

children.title: Runtime Authorization
children.summary: Explains runtime authorization in Cúram, including how FIDs are added to groups and published, how field-level SIDs mask unauthorized field values with asterisks, and how user-defined SIDs extend security to custom securable elements via `curam.util.security.Authorisation.isSIDAuthorised()`. It also notes that authorization checks occur on both client and server, with client-side checks happening before page load and configurable in `curam-config.xml`.

children.title: Client Authorization Checks
children.summary: Describes client-side authorization checks in Cúram runtime security. Before a page loads, the web client verifies whether the user can access the requested method or field; if not, access is blocked and the server is not called. The feature is configurable in curam-config.xml. This section appears under runtime authorization and is useful for understanding where authorization is enforced and how client-side checks can prevent server invocation.

children.title: Server Authorization Checks
children.summary: Server-side authorization checks provide a second layer of access control in Cúram, used for non-web-client access or when client-side checks are disabled. Unlike client authorization failures, server failures are always logged. Failed authorizations are stored in the AuthorisationLog database table with user name, security identifier, and timestamp for audit purposes.

Overview of Cúram authorization: external access authentication hooks, custom verifications, and how access is granted/denied to functional elements via SIDs. Explains the security model of roles, groups, and SID mappings, including BASESECURITYGROUP and cached security data. Covers SID types: FIDs for facade/BPO methods, field-level SIDs that mask unauthorized values as "***", and user-defined SIDs via `Authorisation.isSIDAuthorised()`. Also introduces runtime authorization, including client-side prechecks before page load and server-side checks.

children.title: 1.3 Cryptography in Cúram
Ciphering
children.summary: Explains Cúram’s cryptography basics: ciphering encrypts plain text with an algorithm and secret key, and Cúram encrypts passwords for secrecy, stores multiple encrypted passwords in property files, and decrypts them when needed (for example, database access). Also covers digesting/hashing as a one-way process used for some user passwords in authentication. Describes CryptoConfig.properties and related keystore/salt files as critical security components, warns to protect and regularly update them, and advises against using default cryptography settings in production.

children.title: Digesting
children.summary: This section explains digesting (hashing) in Cúram: a one-way cryptographic process that creates a fixed-length digest from input data, preventing recovery of the original input. It notes that passwords not requiring encryption are stored as digests for authentication. It also introduces cryptography configuration via CryptoConfig.properties, including related keystore and salt files, and warns that default settings are for development/testing only and should not be used in production.

children.title: Cryptography Properties
children.summary: This section explains Cúram cryptography basics: ciphering (encryption) of passwords with an algorithm and secret key, and digesting (hashing) as a one-way process for user authentication. It notes that encrypted passwords in property files are decrypted when needed, while digests cannot be reversed. It also describes CryptoConfig.properties and related keystore/salt files as critical security components, recommends strict access controls and regular changes/segmentation, and warns not to use the default cryptography configuration in production.

children.title: Cipher Settings
children.summary: Explains Cúram cipher settings for encrypting passwords in property files/configurations. Lists CryptoConfig.properties options: cipher algorithm (default AES, FIPS-compliant), optional superseded algorithm for migration, keystore location and store password for the secret key, and optional JCE provider class. Notes OOTB settings may suit development, but production should customize and rotate the cipher secret key regularly. Also says these defaults are enabled by default and may require changes during upgrades.

children.title: Digest Settings
children.summary: Explains Cúram’s password digest settings for form-based login (not used for LDAP/third-party auth), where passwords are transformed into stored digests instead of plain text. Recommends customizing default crypto settings for production, using salts and iterations to strengthen hashes. Lists configurable properties in CryptoConfig.properties: digest algorithm (default SHA-256), optional encrypted salt file, and iteration count. Also describes “superseded” digest properties for migration support, controlled by the convertsupersededpassworddigests.enabled setting.

children.title: Cipher-Encrypted Passwords
children.summary: Lists Cúram passwords that are cipher-encrypted and where they appear: Bootstrap.properties, AppServer.properties, Application.prx, web services config files, and CTM target system passwords. It names key properties such as curam.db.password, security.password, async credential, DB-to-JMS, web service, LDAP, SMTP, and workspace protection key. It also introduces Security Data Caching, explaining that authorization data from user/role/group tables is cached to speed authorization and affects login authentication via the WebSphere cache.

Introduces Cúram cryptography as two mechanisms: ciphers for two-way password encryption and digests for one-way password hashing, mainly for login authentication. Explains that CryptoConfig.properties controls both cipher and digest behavior, with related keystore and salt files being security-critical. Notes AES and SHA-256 as default/FIPS-compliant algorithms, supports superseded settings for migration, and warns that out-of-the-box settings are for development/testing only and should be customized and protected in production.

children.title: 1.4 Security Data Caching
Cúram Security Cache
children.summary: Explains the Cúram Security Cache, which stores a user’s authorization data to improve search and retrieval during authorization. Notes that security information from database tables supporting users, roles, and groups is cached by the infrastructure. The section also introduces “Security Data Caching” and references the WebSphere cache and its effect on user authentication at login.

children.title: Cache Refresh
children.summary: The security cache is loaded on demand, shared, and inaccessible directly from application code except through the authorization interface for queries. If case sensitivity is disabled, usernames are stored and queried in uppercase, and duplicate case-insensitive usernames cause a fatal initialization error. Security data changes require an asynchronous cache refresh, triggered by reboot or sysadmin action; failure can be hard to detect, so a callback for refresh failure feedback is recommended. WebSphere also caches credentials for 10 minutes, delaying enforcement of password or account restriction changes.

children.title: Cache Refresh Failure
children.summary: Explains that Cúram’s security cache is refreshed asynchronously whenever security tables change, typically on application reboot or via sysadmin action. Because refresh failures give no direct feedback, administrators may need logs or manual checks; the section recommends implementing the optional cache refresh failure callback interface to report reload errors. It also notes WebSphere’s separate security cache, which can delay account restriction or password changes from taking effect until cached credentials expire.

children.title: WebSphere Caching Behavior
children.summary: WebSphere caches user credentials and authentication data separately from Cúram’s security cache. While a user remains valid in the WebSphere security cache, the Cúram login module is not called. The default invalidation period is 10 minutes of inactivity. This means changes to a user’s account restrictions or password may not take effect until that WebSphere cache entry expires or is invalidated.

Explains Cúram’s security data caching for authorization. The Cúram Security Cache stores user/role/group authorization data, loads on demand, is shared, and is only queryable via the authorization interface. It covers case-insensitive usernames and fatal errors from duplicate normalized names. Security table changes require asynchronous cache refresh, typically on reboot or sysadmin action; failed refreshes are hard to detect, so a callback is recommended. Also describes WebSphere’s separate credential cache, which can delay password or account restriction changes for up to 10 minutes of inactivity.

children.title: 1.5 Security for Alternative Clients
Mandatory Cúram Users
children.summary: Describes mandatory Cúram user accounts that must exist for authentication-dependent processes such as JMS messaging, batch processing, web services, deferred processing, and workflow. Key default users include SYSTEM, DBTOJMS, and WEBSVCS; usernames are case sensitive and default credentials should be changed. Explains that web services require manual authorization via security groups/roles and notes Apache Axis2 and Rampart-related security considerations. Batch Launcher authentication is also mentioned, with application-level auth not performed there.

children.title: Web Services
children.summary: Explains security requirements for Cúram alternative clients, especially web services. Notes that processes like web services must authenticate with a valid user, and introduces mandatory users such as SYSTEM, DBTOJMS, and WEBSVCS. For web services, Apache Axis2 uses default credentials that can be changed globally or per service, but the default WEBSVCS user is not authorized by default. Access requires manual association of the service with a security group and role linked to the user; Rampart encryption is also mentioned.

children.title: Batch Processing
children.summary: Batch Processing explains security for non-web alternative clients in Cúram, especially batch jobs. It notes that batch processing must use a valid Cúram user because all application-interacting processes require authentication. The section highlights the mandatory DBTOJMS user, the default user for Database to JMS triggers used by batch processing, and reminds that usernames are case sensitive and default credentials should be changed. It also states that the Batch Launcher does not perform application-level authentication or authorization because it runs without the application server.

children.title: JMS Messaging
children.summary: This section explains JMS Messaging in Cúram: JMS messages are used by deferred processes and Workflow and require valid Cúram credentials. The SYSTEM user must exist, be enabled, and have the SYSTEMROLE role; its default locale is used for JMS messages. It also notes that the SYSTEM username can be changed during or after deployment, with details in the Server Developer’s Guide.

children.title: Deferred Processing
children.summary: Deferred processes in Cúram are asynchronous business methods that interact with the application and therefore require valid Cúram credentials. The SYSTEM user must exist in the Users table, be enabled, and have the SYSTEMROLE role; its default locale is used for deferred process locale. For offline unit testing, the username is blank and the effective locale is the server default. This section also sits near related notes on JMS messaging and DB-to-JMS transfer credentials.

Explains security for alternative clients that operate without a logged-in web user, including batch processing, web services, deferred processing, JMS, and workflow. These processes still require valid Cúram user accounts, with mandatory case-sensitive defaults such as SYSTEM, DBTOJMS, and WEBSVCS; changing default credentials is recommended. Web services using Axis2 require manual authorization via security groups/roles, and Rampart encryption is noted. Batch Launcher only authenticates, without application-level auth.

children.title: 1.6 External User Applications
External User Applications
children.summary: Explains how to build and deploy Cúram applications for external users. Required elements are a separate external user EAR/web client, a custom logon.jsp passing user_type for external login, and a custom class extending PublicAccessUser and implementing ExternalAccessSecurity. It also distinguishes internal vs. external users, notes that external users can have different subtypes (for finer-grained authorization), describes how user type is determined via Security Cache or custom UserScope, and mentions deployment security applies at the application-server level.

children.title: User Scope
children.summary: Cúram distinguishes internal and external user scopes. By default, the Security Cache determines scope: users in the cache are internal; others are external, and usernames must be unique. A custom UserScope interface can override this logic. For external users, ExternalAccessSecurity.getSecurityRole() is used for authorization instead of internal roles. The section also notes external-user application requirements: a separate EAR/web client, custom logon.jsp passing user_type, and a custom PublicAccessUser/ExternalAccessSecurity implementation.

children.title: Deployment of an External Application
children.summary: Explains how to deploy external Cúram applications and configure authentication when internal and external apps share an application server. Key points: external apps need a separate EAR, custom logon.jsp with user_type, and a PublicAccessUser implementation plus ExternalAccessSecurity. Covers internal vs external user detection via Security Cache or UserScope, different external user types, and JAAS/AppServer.properties settings such as curam.security.user.registry.disabled.types and enabled.types to control whether the app server registry or Cúram authenticates each type.

This section explains how to build and deploy Cúram applications for external users. External users require a separate EAR/web client, a custom logon.jsp passing user_type, and a PublicAccessUser class implementing ExternalAccessSecurity. It defines internal vs external user scope, with scope determined by the Security Cache or a custom UserScope interface, and notes that external users can have subtypes for finer-grained authorization. It also covers deployment when internal and external apps share an application server, including JAAS/AppServer.properties settings for registry-based authentication.

children.title: 1.7 Configuring Single Sign On (SSO)
Configuring SAML SSO
Configuring SAML SSO on Kubernetes
Cúram SSO on Kubernetes initiation and flow
Configure SAML SSO for Cúram on WebSphere® Liberty
children.summary: Explains how to configure SAML SSO for Cúram on WebSphere Liberty. It outlines enabling the `samlWeb-2.0` and `appSecurity-2.0` features, downloading SAML metadata for the IdP/SSO provider, and adding a `samlWebSso20` configuration with `authFilter` rules in `server.xml`. Example settings include IdP metadata path, SP host/port, LTPA cookie behavior, and disabling signed authn requests/assertions. Includes guidance for the SAML login flow and the ACS redirect to the Cúram landing page.

Explains SAML SSO for Cúram on Kubernetes/Liberty, including why browser-based SAML POST flows are used and how IdP-initiated and SP-initiated logins work. Describes the request/response path through the Trust Association Interceptor and Assertion Consumer Service, SAML validation, and redirect to the Cúram landing page with an LTPA2 cookie. Also notes the Universal Access variant. Ends with Liberty setup steps: enable `samlWeb-2.0` and `appSecurity-2.0`, download IdP metadata, and configure `samlWebSso20` plus `authFilter` rules in `server.xml`.

children.title: Universal Access SSO on Kubernetes initiation and flow
children.summary: Explains Universal Access SSO on Kubernetes for Cúram using SAML web SSO. Covers browser POST bindings, why SP/IdP flows differ for single-page apps, and how SAML responses/assertions are signed/encrypted and turned into a JAAS subject. Describes IdP-initiated and SP-initiated flows, including TAI/ACS interception, redirects, AuthnRequest generation, validation, and LTPA2 cookie issuance. Also notes a server.xml spHostAndPort configuration and checking authentication attributes in the Cúram JAAS login module.

children.title: Configuring the Cúram Universal Access Responsive Web Application for SSO
children.summary: Explains how to configure the Cúram Universal Access Responsive Web Application for SAML SSO by setting properties in the React app’s .env file and rebuilding. Includes example guidance for Kubernetes/ISAM-based SAML 2.0 Browser POST SSO, applicable to both IdP-initiated and SP-initiated flows, with extra steps for SP-initiated cases.

children.title: SAML SSO on Kubernetes configuration example using ISAM
Configuring SSO with IBM® Security Access Manager
children.summary: Explains how to configure single sign-on for Cúram using IBM Security Access Manager (ISAM). Covers ISAM prerequisites, then setup steps in the ISAM management console: database configuration, product license activation, loading the LDAP SSL certificate into the trust store, and configuring the ISAM runtime component, all referencing the ISAM 9 Federation Cookbook. children.title: Configuring IBM® Security Access Manager as an IdP
children.summary: Explains how to configure IBM Security Access Manager (ISAM) for Cúram SSO and as an identity provider (IdP). It lists prerequisites such as starting ISAM, logging in as admin, accepting agreements, and following the IBM Security Access Manager 9 Federation Cookbook. Key steps include configuring the ISAM database, product licenses, LDAP SSL trust store, runtime component, and then IdP setup: reverse proxy, SAML 2.0 IdP federation, ISAM configuration, optional ACL policy, and partner configuration with extra advanced settings.

children.title: Adding and enabling users in LDAP
children.summary: Explains how to add LDAP users and enable them for SAML-based SSO. It provides a sample LDIF file for creating OpenLDAP/IBM Security Access Manager runtime entries, including domain, organization, organizational units, and example users such as caseworker and jamessmith with attributes like cn, uid, mail, and userpassword. It then begins the procedure for loading the LDIF into the OpenLDAP Docker container using docker cp.

children.title: Testing IdP-initiated SAML SSO infrastructure
children.summary: Explains how to test IdP-initiated SAML SSO for Cúram with IBM Security Access Manager. children.title: SP-initiated only: Testing SP-initiated SAML SSO infrastructure

Kubernetes SAML SSO example for Cúram using IBM Security Access Manager (ISAM) with SAML 2.0 Browser POST. children.title: Configuring WebSphere® Application Server as a SAML service provider

children.title: Configuring Merative ™ Cúram Universal Access for SSO

children.title: Configuring CORS for Merative ™ Cúram Universal Access

Steps: log in as a system administrator, open System Configurations > Application Data > Property Administration, and set curam.rest.allowedOrigins to the hostnames or IP addresses of the IdP server and the Universal Access web server. Also situates this within SSO setup for Universal Access.

Explains how to configure Merative Cúram Universal Access for SSO. It requires Cúram to already be set up as a SAML service provider, then setting React app .env variables, switching authentication to SSO, and rebuilding the app. It also details CORS setup when Universal Access and the web API are on different hosts, including configuring curam.rest.allowedOrigins in Property Administration with the IdP and web server hostnames/IPs. Includes IdP_URL and ACS_URL format notes and an IBM Security Access Manager example.

children.title: SAML SSO configuration example with IBM® Security Access Manager

children.title: Configuring IBM® Security Access Manager as an IdP
children.summary: Explains how to configure IBM Security Access Manager (ISAM) as an identity provider by following specific sections of the ISAM 9.0 Federation Cookbook: create a reverse proxy, set up a SAML 2.0 IdP federation, configure ISAM for the IdP, optionally apply ACL policy, and configure the partner. Notes that Docker deployments can reuse the container keystore and that WebSphere exports require additional advanced configuration. children.title: Configuring WebSphere® Application Server as a SAML service provider
children.summary: Configures WebSphere Application Server as a SAML service provider for Cúram using the WebSphereSamlSP.ear ACS app. Covers deployment via admin console or wsadmin script, required trust association interceptor settings, IdP partner/trusted realm setup, SSL certificate import, and disabling logoutOnHTTPSessionExpire. Also explains adding CORS support for ACS redirections by implementing a servlet filter and mapping it in web.xml, with the ACS app using the same virtual host as the REST target application.

children.title: Add and enable the users in LDAP

The sample LDIF defines the directory structure and two users: caseworker and jamessmith, with their DNs, attributes, and passwords. It then begins the procedure to copy the LDIF into the Docker-based LDAP container using `docker cp` on the host server. Useful if you need the exact user entries and initial steps for populating the LDAP database.

children.title: Test IdP-initiated SAML SSO infrastructure
children.summary: Explains how to test IdP-initiated SAML SSO for Cúram using IBM Security Access Manager. It covers loading users into OpenLDAP and ISAM, importing and enabling sample users in pdadmin, then hitting a specific IdP-initiated SAML URL to initialize IdP endpoints before logging in via the home page. Notes that ISAM initializes IdP endpoints only on first request, and Universal Access/XHR can timeout if initialization is not completed first.

children.title: SP-Initiated only: Implementing the SAML AuthnRequest functionality in WebSphere® Application Server
children.summary: Explains how to implement SP-initiated SAML web SSO in WebSphere Application Server, which is not supported by default. It instructs developers to implement the com.ibm.wsspi.security.web.saml.AuthnRequestProvider interface and shows a sample SPInitTAI class. The example builds and Base64-encodes a SAML AuthnRequest, sets SSO URL, ACS URL, request ID, issuer, destination, NameID policy, and requested auth context, and references Cúram/ISAM property values used for the IdP and ACS endpoints.

children.title: SP-Initiated only: Test SP-initiated SAML SSO infrastructure
children.summary: How to test SP-initiated SAML SSO infrastructure after configuring WebSphere/ISAM. It covers packaging the AuthnRequestProvider JAR, setting the ACSTrustAssociationInterceptor custom property sso_1.sp.login.error.page to curam.sso.SPInitTAI, and restarting the app server if needed. The test procedure is to open a protected REST URL, confirm redirection to the ISAM login page, sign in with the configured reverse proxy credentials, and verify redirect back to the protected definitions page.

Example end-to-end SAML SSO setup for Cúram Universal Access with IBM Security Access Manager. Covers required architecture, ISAM console setup, configuring ISAM as IdP, WebSphere Application Server as SAML SP/ACS, CORS handling, LDAP user creation/import and enabling, and testing IdP-initiated SSO. Also includes an SP-initiated-only extension for implementing AuthnRequestProvider in WebSphere and testing SP-initiated flow. Useful if you need the concrete configuration steps, sample properties, URLs, and code for ISAM/WebSphere-based SSO.

children.title: Configuring SAML SSO with Microsoft™ Azure

Creating a new custom enterprise application

children.title: SP-Initiated only: Implementing the SAML AuthnRequest functionality in WebSphere® Application Server

Configuring the Cúram Universal Access Responsive Web Application for SSO
