children.title: Creating your Identity Provider partners
children.summary: Explains how to create and configure Identity Provider (IdP) partners for SAML 2.0 single sign-on in WebLogic/Cúram. Covers publishing and distributing SP metadata, obtaining trusted IdP metadata, creating a new Web Single Sign-On IdP partner, enabling the partner, setting description and attribute consumption, and noting read-only signing settings from metadata. Also includes optional name mapping and artifact signing settings, plus configuring redirect URIs (with wildcard/file-type constraints) and SP Binding/Transport options.

Covers configuring Oracle WebLogic Server as a SAML 2.0 service provider for Cúram. Includes ISAM federation prerequisites, creating the SAML2IdentityAsserter, setting SAML 2.0 general services and SP options, publishing SP metadata, and creating/configuring Identity Provider partners from imported metadata. Notes basic defaults like recipient check enabled, replicated cache false for basic setup, all bindings enabled, and optional signing/TLS, name mapping, virtual users, redirect URIs, and binding/transport settings.

children.title: Adding and enabling the users in LDAP
children.summary: Explains how to add and enable LDAP users for ISAM runtime. It shows creating an LDIF file to populate OpenLDAP with the required directory structure and sample users, including a caseworker and an external user with attributes such as cn, sn, uid, mail, and password. It then begins the procedure for loading the LDIF into the OpenLDAP database by copying the file into the LDAP Docker container. Useful if you need the exact user setup steps and sample LDIF format.

children.title: Testing IdP-initiated SAML SSO infrastructure
children.summary: Explains how to test IdP-initiated SAML SSO in IBM Security Access Manager for Cúram. It covers adding users to OpenLDAP, importing them into ISAM, and then opening a browser URL with ISAM login, WebSphere host, ACS, NameIdFormat, and target parameters. The section notes the ISAM container initializes IdP endpoints only on first connection, so a direct test request is needed to avoid Universal Access XHR timeout before finishing initialization, then users should log in via the home page.

children.title: SP-Initiated only: Testing SP-initiated SAML SSO infrastructure
children.summary: Explains how to test SP-initiated SAML SSO infrastructure for a protected REST URL in Oracle WebLogic. The procedure uses a browser with devtools, opens a protected REST endpoint, expects redirection to the ISAM login page, then verifies successful return to the definitions page after authentication. Also notes a related IdP test URL format and defines key federation parameters. Ends with a brief intro to customizing the login module via a custom Cúram JAAS security provider and points to WebLogic/JAAS security references.

Overview of Cúram Universal Access SSO with IBM Security Access Manager using SAML 2.0 Browser POST for both IdP- and SP-initiated flows. Covers component architecture, initial ISAM console setup (database, licenses, SSL trust store, runtime), and how to configure ISAM as an IdP via the Federation Cookbook. Also details WebLogic SAML 2.0 service provider setup, metadata exchange, LDAP user creation/import, and test procedures for IdP-initiated SSO, including endpoint initialization caveats.

children.title: Customizing the login module
JAAS login module support for authentication in a customized solution
children.summary: Explains when the built-in Cúram JAAS login module may be insufficient and how to replace it with a custom JAAS login module. Notes that the sample CuramLoginModule only supports basic username/password auth and that the original module should normally remain with identity-only authentication enabled unless fully replaced. Warns that even if removed, users must still exist in the Cúram Users table and the custom module must trigger Security Cache updates for new users. Includes Oracle WebLogic steps to deregister the existing Cúram security provider and prepare for registering a custom one.

children.title: Replacing the Cúram JAAS login module with a custom login module
De-registering the existing Cúram security provider
children.summary: Explains how to de-register the existing Cúram security provider in Oracle WebLogic when replacing the built-in JAAS login module with a custom one. It warns that although the Cúram login module can be removed, users must still exist in the Cúram Users table and the custom module must trigger Security Cache updates for new users. Includes steps to delete myrealmCuramAuthenticator from the WebLogic Security Realm and advises saving the provider admin credentials for possible reuse.

children.title: Creating and registering a custom security provider
Creating the authentication provider and login module runtime classes
Creating the authentication provider
children.summary: Explains how to create a WebLogic authentication provider for Cúram. It describes exposing the security provider through the WebLogic Security Framework so it can be managed in the Administration Console, then lists required SSPI methods: initialize, getDescription, shutdown, getLoginModuleConfiguration, getAssertionModuleConfiguration, getPrincipalValidator, and getIdentityAsserter. It also introduces creating the JAAS LoginModule using the Cúram login module as a template and outlines key LoginModule methods, including initialize and login.

children.title: Creating the JAAS LoginModule
children.summary: This section explains how an authentication provider exposes security services to Oracle WebLogic Server and can be initialized, started, and stopped via the Administration Console using MBean configuration. It then introduces how to implement the authentication provider SSPI and lists key methods such as initialize, getDescription, shutdown, and configuration accessors. The section specifically begins guidance on creating the JAAS LoginModule, using the Cúram login module as a template, and describes the LoginModule interface methods initialize and login for authentication and principal creation.

Explains how to replace Cúram’s JAAS login module with a custom WebLogic security provider. It says the existing Cúram security provider can be deleted in the WebLogic Administration Console, but the Cúram Users table must still be maintained and the Security Cache updated if the Cúram login module is fully replaced. It then covers creating/registering a custom authentication provider, exposing it through WebLogic Security Framework, and implementing required SSPI methods plus a JAAS LoginModule, using the Cúram login module as a template.

children.title: Generating an MBean type using Oracle WebLogic Server MBeanMaker
children.summary: Explains how to generate and install a custom WebLogic MBean type with MBeanMaker for a Cúram security provider. Covers creating the MBean Definition File (MDF), generating the MBean type and JAR (MJF), classpath requirements, and installing the type into the WebLogic environment. Also gives admin-console steps to configure a custom authentication provider in a security realm after restarting AdminServer, including naming it “myrealmCuramAuthenticator” and selecting type “CustomAuthenticator”.

children.title: Configuring the custom authentication provider using the administration console
children.summary: Explains how to configure a custom authentication provider in WebLogic after installing the MBean type. It instructs restarting AdminServer, opening the Administration Console, and navigating to Security Realms > myrealm > Providers > Authentication. The procedure creates a new authentication provider named “myrealmCuramAuthenticator” of type “CustomAuthenticator” and then selects it in the providers list. The section also notes classpath requirements and links to WebLogic MBeanMaker steps for generating and installing the custom MBean type.

Explains how to replace the Cúram JAAS login module with a custom WebLogic security provider. It notes that if the Cúram login module is removed, users must still exist in the Cúram Users table and the Security Cache must be updated for new users. The section covers implementing the authentication provider SSPI and JAAS LoginModule, generating and installing a custom MBean type with WebLogic MBeanMaker, and configuring the new provider in the WebLogic Administration Console as “myrealmCuramAuthenticator” of type “CustomAuthenticator.”

Explains how to replace Cúram’s built-in JAAS login module with a custom WebLogic authentication solution. Notes that the Cúram module is only a sample, can be removed with Merative support, but users must still exist in the Cúram Users table and the custom module must trigger Security Cache updates for new users. Covers deleting the existing myrealmCuramAuthenticator, implementing the authentication provider SSPI and JAAS LoginModule, generating/installing an MBean type with WebLogic MBeanMaker, and creating a new CustomAuthenticator provider in the WebLogic Administration Console.

Explains how to customize or replace the Cúram JAAS login module in WebLogic. It notes the shipped CuramLoginModule is only a basic username/password sample and should usually remain with identity-only auth unless fully replaced with Merative support. If replaced, users must still exist in the Cúram Users table and the custom module must update the Security Cache for new users. Also covers deleting myrealmCuramAuthenticator, implementing the authentication provider and JAAS LoginModule, generating/installing an MBean type, and registering a CustomAuthenticator provider.

Covers SAML SSO setup on Oracle WebLogic Server for Cúram, including Universal Access specifics. Explains SAML bindings, IdP- and SP-initiated flows, ACS validation, redirects, cookies/session handling, and why SPA-style Universal Access needs 302/POST handling. Describes default Cúram and Universal Access flow diagrams, IBM Security Access Manager test steps, and custom login module replacement in WebLogic, including JAAS/provider/MBean setup and security cache implications.

children.title: Extending the SAML SSO configuration to enable multifactor authentication
SAML SSO example configuration with MFA using IBM® Secure Verify Access
Activating the Advanced Access Control module
children.summary: Explains how to activate IBM Security Verify Access Advanced Access Control (AAC) for use. Notes AAC is an add-on module for advanced authentication/authorization and requires a license activation key from Passport Advantage or IBM Security Systems License Key Center. Procedure starts in the ISVA Local Management Interface: go to System > Licensing and Activation, then import the AAC license text file via Activated Modules > Import. Also notes AAC can be used as the first or second factor in a multi-mechanism authentication policy.

children.title: Publishing the configuration snapshot and reloading the appliances
children.summary: Explains how to publish the configuration snapshot in the LMI and then apply it by reloading ISVA Docker appliances. Steps include selecting Container Management > Publish configuration, submitting to create a snapshot, then using docker ps -a and docker exec ... isam_cli with reload check and reload all for config, AAC, and WebSEAL containers. Also notes the next task: configuring WebSEAL reverse proxy integration with the AAC runtime using the wizard and default values (isam-aac, port 443, eauser, /mga).

children.title: Configuring the reverse proxy integration with the Advanced Access Control module
children.summary: Explains how to configure WebSEAL reverse proxy integration with the Advanced Access Control (AAC) runtime server using the configuration wizard. It directs users to IBM’s reverse-proxy AAC setup steps, with specifics to choose the default reverse proxy instance name and use AAC Runtime tab defaults: host name isam-aac, port 443, username eauser with the default admin password, and junction /mga. This section is useful for setting up the reverse proxy as the AAC point of contact server.

children.title: Configuring the multifactor authentication scenario
Creating and configuring the POP policies for step-up login
children.summary: Explains how to create and configure POP policies in the Local Management Interface for step-up login. It defines three POPs—level0pop, level1pop, and level2pop—used to control access behind WebSEAL by authentication level (0 for unauthenticated resources, 1 for username/password login, 2 for second-factor and protected resources). The section walks through creating the POPs, setting their IP Auth authentication levels, and attaching protected resources to each POP, including attaching level2pop at the root and overriding it lower in the WebSEAL tree.

children.title: Updating the WebSEAL configuration file with all login methods
children.summary: Explains how to update the WebSEAL configuration file so all login methods are supported during step-up authentication. The key setting is adding a [step-up] section with show-all-auth-prompts = yes, so WebSEAL can render the normal form-based login before AAC two-factor authentication completes. It also notes that most second-factor AAC policies need an initial authentication to identify the user’s second-factor details. Briefly introduces a related follow-on procedure for redirecting WebSEAL to AAC for step-up authentication.

children.title: Configuring WebSEAL to redirect to AAC for step-up authentication
children.summary: This section explains how to configure WebSEAL for step-up authentication with AAC. It lists POP resource attachments for unauthenticated, authenticated, and proxied content, then shows how to edit the WebSEAL configuration file so normal form-based login is processed before AAC two-factor login. It includes the `[step-up] show-all-auth-prompts = yes` setting and begins the procedure for redirecting WebSEAL to use the AAC authentication policy for step-up authentication.

children.title: Configuring the email OTP delivery mechanism
children.summary: Explains how to configure the email OTP delivery mechanism in the LMI for AAC authentication. It instructs administrators to navigate to AAC > Policy > Authentication > Mechanisms > email One-time Password, then edit mechanism properties. The example lists required SMTP-related settings: sender email, SMTP hostname, SMTP port, and SMTP username/password. Also notes related config-file validation for authentication levels and local-response-redirect settings for step-up auth.

children.title: Modifying the OTPGetMethods mapping rule
children.summary: How to customize the OTPGetMethods mapping rule in the Local Management Interface so the one-time password can be delivered by email. Covers navigating to AAC > Policy > Authentication > Advanced, editing OTPGetMethods, and updating the email OTP method in the sample JavaScript snippet. Notes that the maskEmail function is optional depending on whether the user’s email should be shown on the OTP form.

children.title: Testing the multifactor authentication scenario
children.summary: This section explains how to modify the OTPGetMethods mapping rule in ISVA’s Local Management Interface so one-time passwords can be delivered by email. It includes navigation steps in AAC > Policy > Authentication > Advanced, then editing OTPGetMethods and adding an email delivery method using the user’s email address. It also notes SMTP-related settings (Use SSL, STARTTLS, TLS Protocol) and that masking the email shown on the OTP form is optional.

children.title: Updating the AuthSvcCredential mapping rule
children.summary: Explains how to update the AuthSvcCredential mapping rule so AUTHENTICATION_LEVEL is set to 2 after an AAC authentication policy, preventing endless login redirect loops in SP-initiated SAML SSO MFA flows. Notes this is only needed for custom OTP mechanisms, not IBM Secure Verify Access default OTP methods. Mentions checking the poc.otp.authLevel advanced configuration property in LMI and gives the path to edit the rule via AAC > Global Settings > Advanced Configuration and AuthSvcCredential > Edit.

Describes how to configure a WebSEAL/AAC multifactor login flow: create POPs for auth levels 0/1/2 and attach resources, enable `[step-up] show-all-auth-prompts = yes`, set redirect settings to send step-up auth to AAC MAC-OTP, configure the email OTP mechanism (SMTP properties), customize OTPGetMethods to deliver OTP by email, and test the SP-initiated SAML SSO flow. Also notes AuthSvcCredential may need updating so AUTHENTICATION_LEVEL becomes 2 and avoids redirect loops.

Walks through a Cúram SAML SSO MFA example using IBM Security Verify Access as IdP. Covers prerequisites, activating the AAC module with a license, publishing/reloading ISVA containers, and configuring WebSEAL reverse proxy integration to AAC runtime (/mga, isam-aac, eauser). Then shows how to build step-up MFA with POPs, WebSEAL config changes, email OTP delivery, OTPGetMethods customization, testing SP-initiated SSO, and updating AuthSvcCredential to avoid redirect loops.

Explains how to extend Cúram SAML SSO to support MFA using IBM Secure Verify Access. Covers the reference setup, prerequisites, activating the AAC module, publishing/reloading ISVA containers, and configuring WebSEAL reverse proxy integration with AAC runtime (/mga, isam-aac, eauser). Then details a step-up MFA flow using POPs, WebSEAL config changes, email OTP delivery and OTPGetMethods customization, SP-initiated test steps, and a note on updating AuthSvcCredential to prevent redirect loops.

This section explains SAML 2.0 SSO for Cúram, mainly on WebSphere Liberty/Kubernetes, with IdP-initiated and SP-initiated browser POST flows, ACS/TAI validation, RelayState, assertions, and LTPA2 cookie handling. It includes Liberty configuration samples, SAML metadata setup, and JAAS subject checks, plus Universal Access configuration notes. It also covers WebLogic/WebSphere variants, ISAM/Azure examples, and an extension to MFA using IBM Security Verify Access, including POPs, WebSEAL step-up, email OTP setup, mapping rules, testing, and redirect-loop avoidance.

children.title: Configuring SSO
Configuring SSO by using IBM® WebSphere® Application Server LTPA
children.summary: Explains how Cúram token-based SSO works on IBM WebSphere Application Server using LTPA and custom JAAS login modules. Describes that an LTPA token is created after credentials are added, then reused to identify the user in SSO, and recommends inserting a custom login module in the chain. Notes the Cúram login module checks for an existing valid LTPA token via WSCredTokenCallbackImpl and skips authentication if found. Also mentions credentials are not added when curam.security.check.identity.only is true.

children.title: Configuring SSO by using Oracle WebLogic Server WL_Token
children.summary: Explains how to configure SSO with Oracle WebLogic Server using WL_Token. For WebLogic, the Cúram JAAS login module must not add credentials to the JAAS subject so the configured authentication provider can supply the user’s principals and group membership. This is controlled by curam.security.check.identity.only = true and curam.security.user.registry.enabled = true, with additional fine-grained control via curam.security.user.registry.enabled.types and disabled.types for external users. Also introduces OpenID Connect SSO as the next topic.

Overview of token-based SSO setup for Cúram on WebSphere Application Server and WebLogic Server. On WebSphere, SSO uses LTPA plus custom JAAS login modules; a valid LTPA token lets the Cúram login module skip authentication, and a custom login module is recommended in the chain. Credentials are normally added unless curam.security.check.identity.only=true. The page also introduces WebLogic SSO (via authentication provider/WL_Token) and notes token-based SSO is tested only on Cúram.

children.title: Configuring OpenID Connect SSO
Configuring the OpenID Connect provider application in Microsoft™ Azure
children.summary: Explains how to configure Microsoft Azure as an OpenID Connect provider app for IBM WebSphere Application Server. Covers prerequisites (Azure account, admin role, relying party redirect URI), registering an app in Azure AD, copying the client ID and OIDC metadata endpoint, setting authentication with the Web platform and redirect URI, choosing token types based on flow/response_type, and creating a client secret that must be copied immediately. Useful for setting up Azure as the OIDC provider for a relying party.

children.title: Configuring WebSphere® Application Server as an OpenID Connect relying party
children.summary: Explains how to configure WebSphere Application Server as an OpenID Connect relying party for Cúram SSO with Azure. Covers deploying the WebsphereOIDCRP.ear app (via admin console or wsadmin Python script) and enabling the OIDC trust association interceptor. Lists required custom properties such as client ID/secret, discovery/authorize/token/issuer URLs, intercepted paths, user identifier, signing alias, scope, and response type, with the redirect URL format and Azure metadata source.

Explains OpenID Connect SSO setup for Cúram using Microsoft Azure as the OIDC provider and IBM WebSphere Application Server as the relying party. Covers Azure app registration, copying the client ID and metadata endpoint, configuring web authentication and redirect URI, selecting token types, creating a client secret, and assigning users. Also describes deploying WebsphereOIDCRP.ear and enabling the OIDC trust association interceptor in WebSphere, including key custom properties such as client/secret, discovery/authorize/token/issuer URLs, intercepted paths, user identifier, signing alias, scope, and response type.

This section explains how to configure SSO for Cúram across supported platforms: SAML 2.0 browser SSO on Kubernetes/WebSphere Liberty (including IdP- and SP-initiated flows, ACS/TAI handling, RelayState, assertions, and LTPA2 cookies), token-based SSO on WebSphere Application Server and WebLogic (JAAS/login-module behavior and credential handling), and OpenID Connect SSO with Microsoft Azure as provider and WebSphere as relying party. It includes sample server settings, metadata/endpoint setup, and custom OIDC properties.

children.title: 1.8 Other Security Considerations
SSL settings for the application
children.summary: Covers SSL defaults and configuration for the Cúram web application. SSL is enabled by default for client and application-server access via web.xml and Cúram configuration scripts, but certificates must still be updated and SSL can be disabled if required (not recommended). Also notes secure-environment considerations (e.g. FIPS 140-2, DB-to-JMS SSL property, Word Integration/TLS v1.2 and SP800-131a jar signing). Ends with a warning about client HTML error pages and exposing Java stack traces only in development, not production.

children.title: Using Cúram in a secure environment
children.summary: Explains how to run Cúram securely: SSL is enabled by default for the web app and application servers via configuration scripts, but certificates must still be updated and SSL can be turned off only if needed. Notes secure-server/FIPS use cases and special requirements for DB-to-JMS notification SSL, Word Integration with TLS v1.2 and SP800-131a-compliant jars, including the enable-sha-2-signed-jars build property. Also warns that client HTML error pages should not expose Java stack traces in production due to injection risk.

children.title: Client HTML error pages
children.summary: Explains how client-side errors are shown as HTML error pages and that, for debugging, development environments may include Java exception stack traces. Warns this should not be enabled in production because those pages are not covered by malicious-code/filtering checks and could increase exposure to injection attacks such as XSS and link injection.

children.title: Enabling HTTP verb permissions
children.summary: Explains how to mitigate HTTP verb tampering by configuring the web server to allow only approved verbs, since Cúram’s supported Java EE version cannot use deployment-descriptor verb restrictions. Provides step-by-step setup for IBM HTTP Server and Oracle HTTP Server, including editing specific conf files, restarting the server, and verifying that nonpermitted verbs are blocked. Includes a mod_rewrite example that allows GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, and OPTIONS and returns 403 for others.

Covers additional security guidance for Cúram: default SSL protection for web/app-server access, certificate updates, and optional SSL disablement; secure-environment concerns such as FIPS, DB-to-JMS SSL, and Word Integration/TLS v1.2/SP800-131a jar signing; warnings against showing Java stack traces in client HTML error pages in production; and how to reduce HTTP verb tampering by configuring IBM or Oracle HTTP Server to allow only approved verbs via mod_rewrite rules.

children.title: 1.9 Customizing Authentication
Customizing the Login Page
children.summary: Explains how to customize Cúram’s login page by copying the default logon.jsp from the CDEJ into a custom web client component’s WebContent folder, and notes that required elements must remain in place. Also covers styling the login page via CSS in the custom component and, for WebLogic Server only, adding the j_character_encoding hidden field to support usernames/passwords with extended characters.

children.title: Applying Styling to the Login Page
children.summary: This section says the login page styling can be customized by adding the appropriate CSS to any stylesheet in the custom web client component. It applies to the copied `logon.jsp` used for the Cúram login screen. It points readers to the Web Client Reference Manual for styling details and for what parts of `logon.jsp` must be preserved.

children.title: Enabling Usernames With Extended Characters for WebLogic Server
children.summary: Explains how to enable extended-character usernames/passwords on WebLogic Server by adding the proprietary `j_character_encoding` hidden field with value `UTF-8` to the `logon.jsp` form (within the table element). Notes this is only needed when using WebLogic Server; otherwise the section can be ignored.

children.title: Changing the Case-Sensitivity of the Username
children.summary: Explains how to control username case sensitivity via the curam.security.casesensitive property in Application.prx (true by default; false makes authentication/authorization ignore case). Also describes adding custom authentication checks by implementing curam.util.security.CustomAuthenticator and configuring its class in curam.custom.authentication.implementation. Notes how custom authentication affects Users table fields, and that identity-only authentication skips those updates. Includes where to enable identity-only authentication using curam.security.check.identity.only in AppServer.properties or the application server console.

children.title: Adding Custom Verifications to the Authentication Process
children.summary: This section explains how to add custom authentication checks by implementing the `curam.util.security.CustomAuthenticator` interface and its `authenticateUser()` method. It describes expected return values from `SECURITYSTATUS` (success must be `LOGIN`), how authentication results affect the Users table (failed logins, account disablement, last successful login), and notes that identity-only authentication does not update those fields. It also covers configuring the custom authenticator in `Application.prx` and enabling identity-only authentication via `AppServer.properties` or the application server console.

children.title: Configuring the Custom Authenticator
children.summary: Explains how to configure a custom authenticator in Cúram. It covers changing username case-sensitivity via curam.security.casesensitive, implementing curam.util.security.CustomAuthenticator and its authenticateUser() method, and how results should use SECURITYSTATUS values. It also notes how authentication outcomes update the Users table unless identity-only authentication is enabled, and how to enable identity-only authentication using curam.security.check.identity.only in AppServer.properties or via the application server console.

children.title: Configuring Identity Only Authentication
children.summary: This section explains how to configure identity-only authentication and related security options. It covers changing username case sensitivity via `curam.security.casesensitive`, adding custom authentication checks by implementing `curam.util.security.CustomAuthenticator`, and configuring the custom authenticator in `Application.prx`. It also notes that custom verification normally updates user login fields, but these are not updated when identity-only authentication is enabled. Identity-only mode is enabled by setting `curam.security.check.identity.only` to true in `AppServer.properties` before configuration or later through the application server console.

children.title: Adding the Cache Refresh Failure Callback Interface
children.summary: Explains how to add a cache refresh failure callback by implementing `curam.util.security.SecurityCacheFailureCallback` in a class with a public default constructor, then registering it via `curam.security.cache.failure.callback`. If unset, no callback runs. Also covers turning off SSL for the client/application: changing `web.xml` transport-guarantee from `CONFIDENTIAL` to `NONE` and configuring non-secure access in application servers, especially WebSphere, either by adding the existing HTTP port 9082 or reusing SSL port 9044 as non-secure.

children.title: Turning off SSL settings for the application
children.summary: SSL is enabled by default for Cúram access. This section explains how to turn SSL off for the application if required by project configuration: change the web.xml transport-guarantee from CONFIDENTIAL to NONE to allow HTTP in addition to HTTPS, and configure the application server accordingly. For WebSphere, the recommended method is to use the existing non-secure port (9082) by adding a host alias, or alternatively reuse SSL port 9044 as non-secure by changing the transport chain to WebContainer.

children.title: Modifying the web.xml File for the Client Application
children.summary: This section explains how to add a cache refresh failure callback and how to disable or relax SSL for the Cúram client. It shows that the callback must implement `curam.util.security.SecurityCacheFailureCallback` and be registered via `curam.security.cache.failure.callback`. For web access, it describes changing `web.xml` `transport-guarantee` from `CONFIDENTIAL` to `NONE` to allow HTTP in addition to HTTPS. It also outlines WebSphere configuration options: use the default non-secure port 9082 (recommended) or repurpose SSL port 9044 as non-secure.

children.title: Modifying the Application Server Configuration
children.summary: Explains how to add a security cache failure callback and how to change SSL-related access settings. The callback must implement `curam.util.security.SecurityCacheFailureCallback`, have a public default constructor, and be registered via the `curam.security.cache.failure.callback` property. It also covers turning off SSL for the client via `web.xml` (`transport-guarantee` from `CONFIDENTIAL` to `NONE`) and two WebSphere server options for HTTP access: add the non-secure 9082 host alias (recommended) or reuse SSL port 9044 as non-secure.

children.title: Analyzing the AuthenticationLog Database Table
children.summary: This section explains the AuthenticationLog database table, which records all authentication attempts, successful or failed. It describes key fields such as timeEntered, userName, altLogin, loginFailures, lastLogin, and loginStatus. It also lists the possible loginStatus values, including LOGIN, BADPWD, BADUSER, ACCDISABLE, ACCEXPIRED, PWDEXPIRED, BREAKIN, RESTRICTED, LOGEXPR, AUTHONLY, and AMBIGUOUS. It notes that the LogAdmin API can be used to query this table and refers readers to Java documentation for details.

Covers Cúram authentication customization. Explains how to copy and style the login page (`logon.jsp`), including a WebLogic-only `j_character_encoding` field for extended-character usernames/passwords. Describes username case sensitivity via `curam.security.casesensitive`, custom authentication via `CustomAuthenticator`/`authenticateUser()`, and identity-only authentication via `curam.security.check.identity.only`. Also includes configuring a cache refresh failure callback, disabling/loosening SSL in `web.xml` and application server settings, and the `AuthenticationLog` table plus its status values and LogAdmin API.

children.title: 1.10 Customizing Authorization
Creating Authorization Data Mapping
children.summary: Explains how to set up Cúram authorization data using DMX files or Administration screens. It outlines the steps for defining a security role, security group, and security identifier (SID), then linking roles to groups in the security tables. It specifies the target DMX files and initial data directory location under %SERVER_DIR%/components/<custom>/data/initial, and notes that security data is essential for application setup.

children.title: Creating a New Security Role
children.summary: Explains how to create a new Cúram security role as part of authorization setup. The section says authorization data can be configured via DMX files or Administration screens, but the examples use initial data files. It describes adding a SecurityRole entry with the rolename attribute in SecurityRole.dmx under %SERVER_DIR%/components/<custom>/data/initial. It also covers related setup steps: creating security groups, linking roles to groups, and creating security identifiers (SIDs) with sidname and sidtype.

children.title: Creating a New Security Group
children.summary: Explains how to create a new security group in Cúram authorization setup. A security group is added by creating a SecurityGroup database entry with the groupname attribute, typically via the SecurityGroup.dmx file in %SERVER_DIR%/components/<custom>/data/initial. This section sits within broader authorization data mapping guidance, alongside creating roles, linking roles to groups, and defining SIDs. Useful for understanding how security groups are defined and populated in initial security data.

children.title: Linking the Security Group to the Security Role
children.summary: Explains how to link a security group to a security role in Cúram authorization setup. A role must be associated with a group by creating a SecurityRoleGroup table entry with rolename and groupname, typically via the SecurityRoleGroup.dmx file under %SERVER_DIR%/components/<custom>/data/initial. This section sits within broader guidance on creating authorization data, including roles, groups, and SIDs for initial security configuration.

children.title: Creating the Security Identifier (SID)
children.summary: Explains how to customize Cúram authorization data by defining security roles, groups, and their mappings, typically via DMX files in the component’s `data/initial` directory or Cúram Administration screens. For this section specifically, creating a new Security Identifier (SID) requires adding an entry to the `SecurityIdentifier` table with `sidname` and `sidtype`. Useful for understanding where SIDs fit in the authorization setup and what database fields must be populated.

children.title: Linking the Security Group to the SID
children.summary: Explains how to link a security group to a SID by adding a SecurityGroupSID entry with groupname and sidname in SecurityGroupSID.dmx under %SERVER_DIR%/components/<custom>/data/initial. It also references related security setup steps: linking roles to users, loading DMX files via Data Manager, and automatic FID generation for facade methods. FIDs use the format <classname>.<methodname>, are stored in <ProjectName>_Fids.xml, and can have security enabled false to skip authorization checks.

children.title: Linking the Security Role to the User
children.summary: Explains how to associate authorization data with a user by updating the user’s entry in Users.dmx to set the rolename matching SecurityRole. Also notes the broader security data-loading flow: security info is defined in DMX files under components/<custom>/data/initial and loaded with the Data Manager. Includes related guidance on FID generation for facade methods, naming conventions (<classname>.<methodname>), storage in <ProjectName>_Fids.xml, and how the security-enabled flag controls authorization checks.

children.title: Loading Security Information onto the Database
children.summary: Explains how to load security-related database data using DMX files and the Data Manager. It covers creating SecurityIdentifier.dmx for SIDs, SecurityGroupSID.dmx to link security groups to SIDs, and updating Users.dmx to assign roles to users. It also introduces function identifiers (FIDs): facade methods automatically generate SIDs/FIDs, stored in <ProjectName>_Fids.xml and loaded to the database, with naming rules, 100-character limit, and a security-enabled flag that can disable authorization checks.

children.title: Creating Function Identifiers (FIDs)
children.summary: This section explains how function identifiers (FIDs) are created for publicly accessible methods marked <<facade>>. It notes that security is automatically enabled, generating a SID and storing both the SID and fidenabled flag in the database-independent <ProjectName>_Fids.xml file under /build/svr/gen/ddl for later database loading via Data Manager. It defines the FID naming format as <classname>.<methodname>, with a 100-character maximum, and explains that if security is disabled at design time, the FID still exists but no authorization check is performed.

children.title: Switching Security off for a Process Method
children.summary: Explains how to switch off security for a process method by setting Generate_Security to false in the model. A FID is still generated, but its security-enabled flag is false, so no authorization check occurs. Notes this is usually only appropriate for methods like login and warns against disabling authorization in production via curam.security.disable.authorisation. Also covers that FID security info cannot be changed after generation, client authorization failure logging via curam.enable.logging.client.authcheck, and authorizing new SID types with isSIDAuthorised().

children.title: Security Considerations During Development
children.summary: Explains security design choices during development of Cúram applications. Covers disabling security for specific process methods via Generate_Security=false, and warns that turning off curam.security.disable.authorisation is only for design time, never production. Notes FID security flags are fixed after generation, requiring model changes and regeneration to alter them. Also describes client authorization-failure logging via curam.enable.logging.client.authcheck and introduces direct authorization for new SID types using Authorisation.isSIDAuthorised().

children.title: Controlling the Logging of Authorization Failures for the Client
children.summary: Explains how client authorization failure logging is controlled by the curam.enable.logging.client.authcheck property. By default it is false, so web client authorization failures are not recorded. When set to true, failures are logged to the AuthorisationLog database table. This section is useful for understanding whether client-side authorization failures are persisted and where to configure that behavior.

children.title: Authorizing New SID Types
children.summary: Describes a server-side method, curam.util.security.Authorisation.isSIDAuthorised(), for performing authorization directly when introducing new SID types. It is intended for use in classes that manipulate the conceptual element protected by the SID, enabling custom authorization checks beyond generated security settings.

children.title: Analyzing the AuthorisationLog Database Table
children.summary: This section explains the AuthorisationLog database table used to record authorization failures. It identifies the logged fields: timeEntered, userName, and identifierName (the SID or FID involved). It also notes that the LogAdmin API can query this table, with further details in the Java documentation.

Explains how to set up Cúram authorization data using DMX files or Administration screens: create security roles, groups, SIDs, link them via SecurityRoleGroup and SecurityGroupSID, and assign roles to users in Users.dmx under components/<custom>/data/initial, then load with Data Manager. Also covers FID generation for <<facade>> methods, naming (<classname>.<methodname>), 100-char limit, and how Generate_Security=false disables checks. Includes development warning about curam.security.disable.authorisation, client auth-failure logging, and isSIDAuthorised() for new SID types.

children.title: 1.11 Customizing Cryptography
Customizing Cipher Settings
children.summary: Explains how to customize Cúram’s default cipher settings and manage encryption keys. It advises planning and testing, restarting the application for changes to apply, and considering CTM-managed encrypted properties to avoid configuration drift. Steps include editing CryptoConfig.properties, updating/removing CryptoConfig.jar, stopping clients/servers, redeploying via Ant targets, re-encrypting passwords, and verifying related functionality. Also covers using keytool (or equivalent) to create and protect secret keys, and ensuring keytool values match CryptoConfig.properties.

children.title: Managing Keys
children.summary: This section explains how to manage the secret key used to encrypt Cúram passwords. It recommends using JDK keytool (or equivalent) to create, store, and protect the key, with careful decisions about key placement and isolation based on security policy. It also says keytool values must match the corresponding CryptoConfig.properties settings, such as the cipher algorithm (for example, AES). Useful if you need guidance on key creation, storage, or aligning keystore settings with Cúram cipher configuration.

children.title: Creating a Keystore and Secret Key
children.summary: Explains how to create a new Cúram keystore and secret key with JDK keytool, replacing the default keystore and then regenerating CryptoConfig.jar. Maps keytool arguments to CryptoConfig.properties settings, notes the secret key password defaults to the store password, and requires CryptoConfig.properties updates if the keystore name/location changes. Describes the expected folder structure, backing up properties, removing old CryptoConfig.jar, stopping clients/servers, running configtest, and that only jceks keystore type is supported.

children.title: Customizing the Digest
children.summary: Explains how to customize Cúram digest settings, including the need to plan carefully, test changes, and restart the application for them to take effect. Notes coordination with Configuration Transport Manager data to avoid out-of-sync systems. Describes how to add a digest salt by encrypting a random string, storing it in a file, and pointing CryptoConfig.properties to it. Also introduces use of superseded digest settings to migrate existing passwords to new crypto settings over a controlled period; this does not apply to LDAP-managed users.

children.title: Specifing a Digest Salt
children.summary: Explains how to specify a salt for digested passwords to add brute-force protection. Cúram does not provide a default salt; you must create a long random string, encrypt it with Ant, store it in a file, and point CryptoConfig.properties to it via curam.security.crypto.digest.salt.location, updating deployed CryptoConfig.jar files. Notes that digest changes require planning, testing, and application restart, and recommends coordinating with migration steps for superseded digest settings, especially for password migration and CTM-managed data.

children.title: Utilizing the Superseded Digest Settings for a Period of Migration
children.summary: Explains how to migrate existing digested passwords to a new crypto configuration (for example, adding a salt) while temporarily supporting both old and new digest settings. Covers the required outage, updating CryptoConfig.properties with superseded property names, enabling automatic password conversion, setting an upgrade start timestamp, and special handling for users not processed by CuramLoginModule (e.g. WEBSVCS). Also describes ending the migration, resetting unchanged passwords, and why superseded conversion should not remain enabled indefinitely.

children.title: Modifying Your Cryptography Configuration for a Production System
children.summary: Explains how to harden Cúram cryptography for production by replacing the default OOTB configuration. Covers creating a new keystore and secret key, re-encrypting all cipher-encrypted property file passwords, testing changes, updating digest settings (salt, iterations, algorithm), and using superseded digest settings during migration. Also stresses regular secret-key rotation and notes the keystore is stored in CryptoConfig.jar under the JRE lib/ext directory. Useful for production security planning and migration compatibility.

Explains how to customize Cúram cryptography for production: update cipher settings, manage/create keystores and secret keys with JDK keytool, regenerate CryptoConfig.jar, and re-encrypt affected property passwords. Also covers digest customization, adding a digest salt, and using superseded digest settings to migrate existing passwords safely. Notes CTM-managed data may need coordination, changes require restart/testing, only jceks keystores are supported, and secret-key rotation is recommended.

children.title: 1.12 Customizing External User Applications
Creating an External User Application
children.summary: Explains how to customize external user applications in Cúram. Because external users are handled differently from internal users, a separate web client application is required. It describes creating a new external login page by copying and modifying logon.jsp to include a hidden user_type field set to EXTERNAL (not INTERNAL). It also covers automatic login for open-access external apps, using a hard-coded username/password, and warns about session management implications.

children.title: Creating an External User Client Login Page
children.summary: Explains how to customize external user applications in Cúram, which require a separate web application from internal users. It describes creating a new external user login page by copying the default logon.jsp into the custom web client and adding a hidden user_type field set to EXTERNAL (or another non-INTERNAL value). It also introduces automatic login pages for open-access external apps, using hard-coded credentials, and warns that session management implications must be considered.

children.title: Creating an External User Client Automatic Login Page
children.summary: Explains how to create an automatic login page for external Cúram user applications that do not prompt for credentials. Because authentication cannot be disabled, the page should auto-submit hardcoded `j_username` and `j_password` values to `j_security_check`, with `user_type` set to `EXTERNAL`. It warns this should be limited to true open-access apps and notes session management may need to be turned off so automatically logged-in users do not retain prior tabs/navigation. Includes a sample JSP script and cautions that vendor/server differences may affect it.

children.title: Extending the Public Access User Class
children.summary: Explains how to integrate a custom external-user solution by extending the abstract `curam.util.security.PublicAccessUser` class and implementing `curam.util.security.ExternalAccessSecurity`. This concrete class is used during authentication and authorization to supply external-user details. The section begins describing the `authenticate()` method, which authenticates external users and is called instead of the configured authentication when a user is identified as external.

children.title: Authenticating an External User
children.summary: Explains how to authenticate an external user by extending `PublicAccessUser` and implementing `ExternalAccessSecurity`. The key method is `authenticate(identifier, password, userType)`, used during external-user login instead of standard authentication, returning a `SECURITYSTATUS` code such as `LOGIN`, `BADUSER`, `BADPWD`, or `PWDEXPIRED`. Notes that external users must support any configured auth mechanism (for example LDAP). Also mentions automatic logging to `AuthenticationLog` and required password-related methods: `upgradeSafePasswordValidation()` and `setPassword()`.

children.title: Determine External User Details
children.summary: Explains how to determine external user login details after authentication using `ExternalAccessSecurity.getLoginDetails(String identifier)`. The method must return a `UserLoginDetails` object containing the user’s application home page code (`APPLICATION_CODE`), default locale, first name, surname, and any informational or warning messages via `InformationalManager`. This data is used to redirect the user to the correct homepage and display banner messages.

children.title: Authorizing an External User
children.summary: Explains how external users are authorized and typed. `getSecurityRole(identifier)` must return the external user’s security role, or `null` if none can be determined so authorization errors are reported correctly. The SDEJ calls it when the user is not in the security cache; external user identifiers must not conflict with internal usernames unless a custom `UserScope` is implemented, or internal access rights may apply. `getUserType(identifier)` identifies whether a user is external and can return specific external user types; internal users always return `INTERNAL`.

children.title: Determining the User Type
children.summary: Explains how `getUserType()` identifies external users during authorization. `curam.util.transaction.TransactionInfo.getProgramUserType()` calls it when a user is not recognized as internal. Internal users always return `"INTERNAL"`, while external users may return a specific external user type, or `"EXTERNAL"` if there is only one type. The section also notes this follows the earlier `getSecurityRole()` authorization flow for external users and requires unique external identifiers to avoid conflicts with internal usernames.

children.title: Preventing the Deletion of a Security Role: Role Usage Count
children.summary: This section covers security-role and external-user helper methods. It explains getRoleUsageCount(), which should return 1+ when a role is referenced so the role cannot be deleted, and notes roles used by any internal or external user must not be removed. It also describes getRegisteredUserName(), which returns the correctly cased username for external users, especially when curam.security.casesensitive is false. The section begins introducing getUserPreferenceSetID() for retrieving an external user’s preference set ID.

children.title: Retrieving a Registered Username
children.summary: Explains `getRegisteredUserName()`, which returns the correctly cased username for an external user, independent of how it was typed at login. The default implementation can simply return the provided username, but if `curam.security.casesensitive` is false, the method may need to normalize case. Notes that all interface methods must handle case-specific requirements when case sensitivity is disabled for external users.

children.title: Reading User Preferences
children.summary: This section covers external user account utilities: preventing deletion of security roles in use via getRoleUsageCount(), normalizing a user’s registered username case via getRegisteredUserName(), and retrieving an external user’s preference set ID via getUserPreferenceSetID(). It emphasizes that roles referenced by any user cannot be removed and that case handling depends on the curam.security.casesensitive setting.

children.title: Modifying User Preferences
children.summary: Explains how external user preferences are retrieved and updated in the security customization interface. Describes `getUserPreferenceSetID(identifier)` for returning the user preference set ID linked to an external user, and `modifyUserPreferenceSetID(userPreferenceSetID, username)` for updating that association. Notes that the implementation should map to the `UserPreferenceInfo` table, and that `curam.custom.externalaccess.implementation` in `Application.prx` must point to the custom class. This property is not dynamic and requires an application restart after changes.

children.title: Configuring External Access Security
children.summary: Explains how to configure external access security by implementing methods to retrieve and update an external user’s user preference set ID. Notes that `getUserPreferenceSetID(identifier)` should return the user preferences set linked to the external user, and `modifyUserPreferenceSetID(userPreferenceSetID, username)` updates that association. The section also states that `curam.custom.externalaccess.implementation` in `Application.prx` must point to the implementing class, and that this property is not dynamic—an application restart is required after changes.

children.title: Determining if a User is Internal or External using the UserScope Interface
children.summary: Explains how to customize internal vs. external user determination via the curam.util.security.UserScope interface. Implement isUserExternal(username) to return true for external users and false for internal users, enabling cases like duplicate usernames across internal and external applications. The implementation class is configured with curam.custom.userscope.implementation in Application.prx and is not dynamic, so the application must be restarted after changes. The method is used during login and authorization checks for secured elements.

children.title: User Type Determination
children.summary: Explains how Cúram determines whether a user is internal or external via the custom `curam.util.security.UserScope` interface. Implement `isUserExternal(String username)` to return true for external users and false for internal ones; it is used during login and security authorization. To plug in a custom implementation, set `curam.custom.userscope.implementation` in `Application.prx` to the class name. The setting is not dynamic and requires an application restart. Also notes this can support different internal/external classifications across deployments.

Explains how to customize Cúram external user applications, which require a separate web client from internal users. Covers creating a custom login page with a hidden `user_type` set to `EXTERNAL`, optional automatic-login JSPs for open-access apps, and session-management cautions. Describes extending `PublicAccessUser` and implementing `ExternalAccessSecurity` for authentication, login details, authorization, role usage, username normalization, and user preferences. Also covers configuring `curam.custom.externalaccess.implementation` and using `UserScope.isUserExternal()` via `curam.custom.userscope.implementation` to distinguish internal vs. external users.

children.title: 1.13 Customizing Sanitization Settings
children.summary: Explains how to customize Cúram’s sanitization allowlist, which filters potentially malicious HTML markup in data and property values. Describes the allowlist file format (tag=attributes), gives examples of safe elements/attributes, and notes that the Rich Text Editor uses a separate allowlist. Covers two customization paths: permanently changing the allowlist by copying and modifying the properties file plus updating AppResource/DMX, or downloading/editing it through the administration UI.

children.title: 1.14 Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) and Cúram
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection for Cúram web pages
children.summary: Explains CSRF protection for Cúram web pages. The UI uses an HTTP referrer header check to validate incoming requests and only allows requests from trusted domains; requests with no referrer are rejected. The key setting is the mandatory curam.referer.domains property, configured in Application.prx or via Cúram system administration. It should be a comma-separated list of allowed domains (for example, abc.com, def.com) and is normally required in deployed environments but not at development time. Includes steps to set and publish the value.

children.title: Cúram web pages navigation scenarios with CSRF protection
children.summary: Explains Cúram v8 CSRF protection for web page navigation and how missing or invalid Referer headers affect requests. Lists scenarios that can trigger redirects: direct URL entry, PDF links, bookmarks, external links, link shorteners, cached pages, and cross-domain requests. Notes that if the user is logged in they are sent to the home/landing page; otherwise to the login page. Describes this as a security tradeoff that may change some previously working navigations.

Explains CSRF protection for Cúram UI pages and related navigation behavior. Cúram uses HTTP Referer header validation to allow only trusted domains; requests with no or invalid referrer are rejected or redirected. The mandatory curam.referer.domains setting is configured in Application.prx or Cúram system administration as a comma-separated list of allowed domains and must be published to take effect. Lists cases that can drop the Referer header—direct URLs, PDFs, bookmarks, external links, link shorteners, cached pages, and cross-domain requests—and notes users are sent to the home/landing or login page.

Overview of Cúram security. Explains default form-based authentication via JAAS over HTTPS/SSL, alternate login IDs, authentication modes (default, identity-only, external access), and how authenticated users are loaded into the Security Cache. Also covers authorization by SIDs/roles/groups, cryptography for password digests/ciphers, security caching, security for alternative clients, external user app setup, SSO options, SSL/production security considerations, authentication/authorization customization, sanitization settings, and CSRF protection via `curam.referer.domains` and referer checks.
